# Lecture Livestreaming in COMP110

## Summary

For this project, I analyzed whether COMP110 could create value for students by offering lecture livestreams or recordings. I chose this idea because the survey asked students if they thought lectures should be livestreamed, and this topic connects to accessibility, flexibility, and reviewing difficult material. I analyzed the survey data from COMP110 students, focusing on the columns for support of livestreaming, course difficulty, core pace, and student understanding. I used data utility functions to read the CSV data, convert it into column-based data, select the relevant columns, count student responses, preview the data, and create visualizations of the data.

<img src="/ilovecomp110/static/imgs/understanding_line.png" alt="Line plot comparing livestream support and average understanding."  width="500"/>

## Conclusion

Overall, my analysis supports the idea that COMP110 could create value by offering lecture livestreams which could then be posted as recordings. The response counts showed that many students gave high ratings in support of livestreaming. The bar chart shows that the rating answered the most was 7 by far, meaning that there is strong student interest in this option. The line plots show that those who are more in favor of adding a livestream also tended to rate the course as more difficult and having a faster pace on average. There is also a trend seen where students who were more in favor of adding a livestream also estimated that they have a lower understanding of the course material on average. This suggests that livestreaming could be especially useful for students who find the course challenging to use as an extra resource or way to not fall behind if they cannot attend class. 

The data is not fully conclusive though because it shows students' opinions and does not necessarily prove that livestreams would improve learning outcomes. There are potential trade-offs because offering livestreams could decrease in-person lecture attendance, students could end up finding that they pay less attention to a livestream than when they are in a classroom setting, and instructors could struggle with managing recording technology and technological issues that might take away from class time. 

I think this idea could be refined by collecting more specific data on livestreams versus recordings. I do believe that lectures should be made more accessible and recommend that COMP110 considers livestreams and recordings. This would satisfy students' wishes for a livestream option, and recordings would provide an additional resource for students to use to study and review that material from class, rather than simply providing a livestream as a way for students to avoid learning in-person.
